message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Ethnopharmacological relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    For millennia in Southeast Asia,
                    <jats:italic>Artemisia annua</jats:italic>
                    L. was used to treat “fever”. This medicinal plant is effective against numerous infectious microbial and viral diseases and is used by many global communities as a source of artemisinin derivatives that are first-line drugs to treat malaria.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim of the Study</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The SARS-CoV-2 (Covid-19) global pandemic has killed millions and evolved numerous variants, with delta being the most transmissible to date and causing break-through infections of vaccinated individuals. We further queried the efficacy of
                    <jats:italic>A. annua</jats:italic>
                    cultivars against new variants.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Materials and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Using Vero E6 cells, we measured anti-SARS-CoV-2 activity of dried-leaf hot-water
                    <jats:italic>A. annua</jats:italic>
                    extracts of four cultivars, A3, BUR, MED, and SAM, to determine their efficacy against five fully infectious variants of the virus: alpha (B.1.1.7), beta (B.1.351), gamma (P.1), delta (B.1.617.2), and kappa (B.1.617.1).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In addition to being effective against the original wild type WA1,
                    <jats:italic>A. annua</jats:italic>
                    cultivars A3, BUR, MED and SAM were also potent against all five variants. IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    and IC
                    <jats:sub>90</jats:sub>
                    values based on measured artemisinin content ranged from 0.3-8.4 μM and 1.4-25.0 μM, respectively. The IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    and IC
                    <jats:sub>90</jats:sub>
                    values based on dried leaf weight (DW) used to make the tea infusions ranged from 11.0-67.7 μg DW and 59.5-160.6 μg DW, respectively. Cell toxicity was insignificant at a leaf dry weight of ≤50 μg in the extract of any cultivar.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Results suggest that oral consumption of
                    <jats:italic>A. annua</jats:italic>
                    hot-water extracts (tea infusions), could provide a cost-effective therapy to help stave off the rapid global spread of these variants, buying time for broader implementation of vaccines.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Artemisia annua hot-water extracts show potent activity in vitro against Covid-19 variants including delta

reference.article-title: Anti-SARS-CoV-2 potential of artemisinins in vitro
